name: Small historic loop of Pau
region_intro: Hiking around Pau offers diverse terrain, situated at the foothills of the Pyrenees Mountains in the New Aquitaine region of France. The landscape features rolling Jurançon hillsides, the Gave de Pau river, and extensive forests like the Bois de Pau. This geographical position provides a range of hiking experiences, from tranquil riverside paths to routes with views of distant mountain peaks. Best hiking trails around Pau The most popular hiking route is Small historic loop of Pau, an easy 2.5 miles (4.0 km) trail that takes 1 hour 4 minutes to complete, exploring the city's historical areas. Another top favourite among local hikers is Over the dam wall loop from Eslourenties-Daban, a moderate 6.1 miles (9.9 km) path. This route features water views and open countryside. Local hikers also love the Notre-Dame de Piétat loop from Pardies-Piétat, a 6.0 miles (9.6 km) trail leading through rural landscapes and past local landmarks, often completed in about 2 hours 42 minutes. Hiking around Pau is defined by Pyrenees foothills, the Gave de Pau river, and Jurançon vineyards. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy urban walks to more challenging routes in the surrounding hills. The routes in Pau are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 700 reviews. More than 6,700 hikers have used komoot to explore Pau's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Pau, and what variety can I expect?
A: There are over 570 hiking routes around Pau, offering a wide range of experiences. You'll find everything from easy strolls along the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2185442" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gave de Pau river&lt;/a&gt; to moderate paths through the Jurançon vineyards, and more challenging treks in the Pyrenean foothills. The region's diverse landscape, from the city's green spaces to the majestic Pyrenees, ensures options for all fitness levels.

Q: Are there any easy, family-friendly walks in Pau?
A: Yes, Pau offers many easy and family-friendly options. For a gentle urban exploration, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e871320546/small-historic-loop-of-pau" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Small historic loop of Pau&lt;/a&gt;, which is 2.5 miles (4.0 km) and takes just over an hour. The city's Parc Beaumont and the banks of the Gave de Pau also provide tranquil walking paths. For more ideas, you can explore family-specific routes on the local tourism site: &lt;a href="https://en.tourismepau.com/walks-and-hikes-in-and-around-pau/family-walks-and-hikes/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Family Walks and Hikes around Pau&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What kind of natural attractions or landmarks can I see while hiking near Pau?
A: Hiking around Pau offers views of the majestic Pyrenees Mountains. You can also explore the picturesque Jurançon hillsides with their vineyards, or the biodiverse wetlands known as the 'Saligues' along the Gave de Pau river. Within the city, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4119942" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palais Beaumont&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4119926" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;National Museum and Estate of the Château de Pau&lt;/a&gt; are notable landmarks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1109052" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boulevard of the Pyrenees&lt;/a&gt; offers panoramic mountain views.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes around Pau?
A: Yes, many routes around Pau are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/35303897" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Over the dam wall loop from Eslourenties-Daban&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 6.1-mile (9.9 km) path featuring water views. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5347883" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Notre-Dame de Piétat loop from Pardies-Piétat&lt;/a&gt;, which is 6.0 miles (9.6 km) through rural landscapes.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Pau?
A: Pau is suitable for hiking year-round, but the best time depends on your preference. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery, with the Jurançon vineyards changing colors. Summer is ideal for higher-altitude hikes in the Pyrenees, though lower trails can be warm. Winter provides opportunities for snowshoeing or enjoying crisp walks in the forests and foothills, with views of snow-capped peaks.

Q: Are there any dog-friendly hiking trails near Pau?
A: Many trails around Pau are suitable for dogs, especially those in natural areas like the Bois de Pau or along the Gave de Pau river. It's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or livestock. Always check local regulations for specific trails or protected areas, such as the Pyrenees National Park, which may have restrictions.

Q: Can I find trails with views of the Pyrenees Mountains?
A: Absolutely. Pau is situated at the foothills of the Pyrenees, so many trails offer stunning mountain vistas. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5346779" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Beautiful view of the Pyrenees – Crête Mathieu Lalanne loop from Jurançon&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route specifically known for its panoramic views. Even within the city, the Boulevard of the Pyrenees provides an iconic backdrop of the mountain range.

Q: Are there any longer, more challenging hikes for experienced hikers?
A: Yes, for experienced hikers, Pau serves as a gateway to more challenging routes. While many local trails are easy to moderate, the broader Pyrenees region, accessible from Pau, offers strenuous, multi-day treks. Within the immediate area, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38610639" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Abbaye de Sauvelade – Rastplatz für Pilger loop from Sauvelade&lt;/a&gt; (over 24 km) or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38612614" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moulin de Louvigny – Compostelle milestone 908km loop from Garos&lt;/a&gt; (over 29 km) provide significant distances and elevation changes.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Pau?
A: The hiking experiences around Pau are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 700 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse scenery, from the tranquil riverside paths and rolling vineyards to the dramatic backdrop of the Pyrenees. The accessibility of trails for various skill levels and the well-maintained paths are also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any hiking trails near Pau that feature waterfalls?
A: While the immediate vicinity of Pau features rivers and lakes, for truly spectacular waterfalls, you would need to venture further into the Pyrenees. The region is home to some of Europe's highest waterfalls, notably in areas like Gavarnie, which is celebrated for its stunning natural beauty and dramatic glacial cirques. These are accessible from Pau for a day trip or longer excursion.

Q: Are there any trails accessible by public transport from Pau?
A: Pau has a local bus network that can take you to trailheads on the city's periphery. For routes further afield, regional buses or trains might be an option, especially for accessing towns in the Pyrenean foothills. It's advisable to check specific route accessibility with the local public transport provider (Idelis for Pau and its agglomeration, or regional services for wider areas) before planning your hike.

Q: Where can I find parking for hiking trails around Pau?
A: Many trailheads around Pau, especially those outside the immediate city center, offer designated parking areas. For urban walks, public parking garages or street parking (which may be paid) are available. When planning your route, check the starting point on komoot or local tourism websites, as they often provide information on nearby parking facilities.
tips: The church of Saint-Martin was first built between 1468 and 1472 on a site opposite the castle of Pau. In the 19th century, the church was too small for the population of Pau and was replaced in 1871 by a new church located a little further east. It was therefore decided to demolish the old church in 1885. The church tower was partially destroyed in 1794, then rebuilt in wood in 1805 before being completely rebuilt when the new Saint-Martin church was built. The new building is of neo-Gothic architecture with a Byzantine influence.

Place Clemenceau is a bit like the heart of the city, a large square where everyone meets.

Teenagers, family, friends, all come to enjoy an ice cream, skate or just sit down to chat on beautiful sunny days.

The Palais Beaumont is Pau’s former Winter Palace, designed by the architect Emile Bertrand and built in 1898 on the site of an old villa bought by the city. The Winter Palace, flanked by two Belle Époque-style bell towers, included a theatre, games rooms, a restaurant and, most notably, a large exotic garden, the Palmarium, covered by a huge elliptical glass roof measuring 1,800 m2.
The building was extremely expensive to maintain, however, and fell into disrepair. It was fully renovated in 1928 by Odon de Béarn, who turned the building into a casino. As part of the renovation works carried out by the architect Georges Wybo, the large glass roof was removed and nine large window doors were installed, which offered lots of light and a view onto the Pyrenees.
Further extensive renovation works were carried out in 2006 by the architect François Lombard. The building was modernised and equipped with conference rooms and became a conference centre.
